THE VICE-CHANCELLOR'S MESSAGE
It is my honour and privilege to welcome you to the Federal University Dutse.
The Student Handbook in the University is an important companion to every student.

It introduces you to the University's history, policies, structures, authorities, rules and
regulations governing student's life and conduct, and provides many other vital
pieces of information that make your stay in the university comfortable.
Students of the Federal University Dutse are engaged in a process of growth and
change. The extent and intensity of each student's involvement in academic work
and other aspects of campus life determines to a large extent the level of individual

success. The campus environment is formative for students and is instrumental in
their effort to achieve academic success and personal fulﬁllment.
Students are, therefore, expected to be hardworking and always strive for excellence
by taking advantage of the facilities and opportunities available in the University.
Similarly, they are enjoined to buy into our core values and directive principles. This
will help create a civilized atmosphere conducive to teaching, learning, research, and

service. It will also equip our students when they graduate to be able to thrive in the
world beyond the four corners of our university.
Students should note that they are ultimately responsible for their own lives and
actions. It is therefore essential that they strive to maintain discipline and uphold the
principles of tolerance in a plural environment such as ours. Students are enjoined to
tackle their problems in a mature and decent manner. The University undertakes to

help our dear students in realizing their legitimate objectives and will only restrain
those who violate the rules or cross the bounds of decency.
We hope to nurture a sound relationship with our students and eventually our alumni
for their mutual beneﬁt and that of the University. I enjoin you to take advantage of
your university years, make friends, and learn from one another—and more
importantly, from your teachers.

importantly, from your teachers.
When you eventually leave FUD as graduates, you shall go forth into the world as
energized and empowered young men and women, who will face the challenges of
life ahead conﬁdently and squarely.
I wish you a pleasant stay at the Federal University Dutse.

Prof. Abdulkarim Sabo Mohammed
Vice-Chancellor
FOREWORD
It is a great honour and privilege for me to present another edition of the Handbook
of the Federal University, Dutse. The Handbook has been revised, many errors of
facts and forms have been corrected, and information has been greatly updated.
The Student Affairs Division of the Federal University Dutse seeks to promote and
facilitate students' development outside the classroom as well as enhancing the

quality of life for the students. The division responds to informational and personal
concerns and oversees the protection of students' rights. The Division also plays a
major role in promoting and upholding the shared values and ideals of the University
Community.
Students are advised to keep themselves well informed by making enquiries at the
Students Affairs Division, their Departments, their Associations, and by developing

the habit of reading notices and write-ups on the Notice Boards and Billboards.
Students should not rely on rumours and hearsay, but should try and conﬁrm every
piece of information from the appropriate quarters.
Furthermore, the contents of this publication should be supplemented with others
such as the Calendar and the Prospectus. The constitution of the Students Union
and other Clubs and Associations also contains some guidance for the students.

A variety of student services such as Advocacy, Student Counseling, Sports and
Recreation, information about scholarships and study grants, student accident
insurance, information for parents and families, absence notices, and other support
services in collaboration with the student union and student clubs and associations
are provided.
The Division provides assistance in times of personal or community crises. Such
assistance includes offering Medical, Psychiatric, and Psychological Services,

arranging for conﬂict resolution through mediation or adjudication, and facilitating
the work of representatives of non-campus agencies.
On behalf of the Students Affairs' Division, I wish to extend our appreciation to the
Vice-Chancellor, Prof. Abdulkarim Sabo Mohammed, all principal oﬃcers, staff of the
Division, the Students Union, and clubs and associations for providing guidance and
support to the Division.
Thank you.
Prof. Ahmad Mohammed Gumel
Dean of Students Affairs

Dean of Students Affairs
CHAPTER ONE: ABOUT THE UNIVERSITY
The Federal University Dutse (FUD) received its charter, along with eight newly
established Federal Universities, from the Federal Government of Nigeria on February
9, 2011. The University's campus is located in the capital of Jigawa State, Dutse, an
ancient town in North-western Nigeria.
The University's host town, Dutse, offers the charm of a small agricultural town, with
easy access to major metropolitan areas such as Kano, Bauchi, Maiduguri, and

Kaduna. The University recognizes the importance of its location in Dutse and seeks
to link its research and teaching to the vast resources of these great metropolitan
areas in the surrounding states.
FUD seeks to attract a diverse and international faculty and student body, to support
research and teaching on global issues, and to create academic relationships with
many universities and higher educational institutions in Nigeria and across the

world. It expects all areas of the university to advance knowledge and learning at the
highest level and to convey the products of its efforts to the community, the nation,
and the world.
As a new Federal Government-owned institution, FUD offers a broad range of degree
programs in the humanities, the Natural and the Social Sciences, Medicine,
Management, Agriculture, and in years to come, courses in Environmental Studies,
Law, Education, and Engineering. FUD's pioneer student body was 265

undergraduates. As a new university, it is committed to the best tradition of
excellence. It aspires to be a teaching and research-intensive, technologically driven
institution. FUD hopes to attract distinguished researchers (nationally and
internationally), authors, scientists, scholars, and other fellows and members of
professional associations and academies, who might help disseminate its vision of
higher education, knowledge, and service.
Vision

Vision
Federal University Dutse shall excel in educating men and women for competence
and character. We will realize this vision by combining teaching and scholarship of
high quality with exemplary leadership at all levels. By doing this, we hope to earn
national and international recognition and further attract human and material
resources needed for sustained and unrelenting excellence.
Mission
Federal University Dutse exists to cultivate learning, develop knowledge, and foster

free exchange of ideas. The University will focus on a strong tradition of
distinguished scholarship, research, teaching, and service that is grounded in a
commitment to increasing and disseminating scientiﬁc, humanistic, and social
knowledge for the beneﬁt of the larger society. In this, FUD will strive to be a major
research university with extensive graduate programmes that are also dedicated to
outstanding undergraduate and professional education.

The University's faculties and departments will be committed to the intellectual,
cultural, and ethical development of students as citizens, scholars, and
professionals. FUD graduates will be prepared to contribute to a global society that
requires leaders with creativity, integrity, and a dedication to service. Finally, FUD is
committed to ideas that would make it recognized around the world as one of the
best institutions of higher education.
Philosophy

Philosophy
The philosophy of FUD is premised on the promotion of an environment in which all
people are inspired to learn, the encouragement of intellectual curiosity, critical
thinking, free inquiry, and respect for the views and values of an increasingly diverse
population. As an academic institution, FUD's philosophy will also be based on
active engagement with addressing the critical needs of the catchment area, nation,

and global community, in close partnership with the host community, the Federal
Government, the Catchment Area, State governments, and with the alumni, and in
partnership with public, private, and non-proﬁt institutions in Jigawa State and
beyond.
Core Values
The core values on which FUD will conduct its affairs are the following:
1. Respect for the fundamental values of human dignity and the right to self-
improvement.

improvement.
2. A commitment to a robust educational environment in which all people are
welcome and feel welcome—one that supports critical thinking, free inquiry,
and respect for diverse views and values. As a community, FUD embraces
diversity as an integral and vital part of everyday life and a cornerstone value
of the University.
3. A commitment to enhancing diversity, equity, and inclusion. Therefore, all or
any acts of discrimination based upon race, colour, ethnicity, gender, class,

age, disability, religion, ancestry, national origin, and other acts of intolerance
are unacceptable at FUD.
4. The consideration that academic honesty and integrity lie at the heart of the
educational enterprise of knowledge production, learning, and service.
Relevance
FUD's relevance as an academic institution consists in its guiding principles that
strongly represent its commitments to the nation and its higher needs, namely:

1. FUD's ﬁrst commitment is to be the ﬂagship of higher education for Nigeria
and the catchment area of the university, ensuring that every Nigerian has
access to a top-quality education and applying our strengths to beneﬁt the
greater Jigawa community. The rising prominence of the Federal University
Dutse will bring a new energy, opportunity, and recognition to the State of
Jigawa, and the nation as a whole.
2. Foster a robust educational environment in which people are welcome and

feel welcome—one that supports critical thinking, free inquiry, and respect for
diverse views and values.
3. Create innovative partnerships for economic and community development,
building knowledge and promoting ideas that serve the critical needs of the
state, the nation, and the world.
4. Engage students, staff, and alumni in the most compelling social, cultural,
artistic, and scientiﬁc challenges of our age—taking our place among the
world's leading universities.

world's leading universities.
FUD's job is not done until our ideas, our expertise, and our students are given the
opportunity to make a signiﬁcant difference in the world. To succeed, we must
ensure that the:
● University's innovation and accomplishments are publicly known across
academia and the world.
● University attracts and supports students from the catchment area, the

nation, and diverse backgrounds.
● University enhances curriculum ﬂexibility, intellectual exploration, and
discovery learning.
● University ensures a multidisciplinary immersion programme that combines
coursework in key subjects engagement.
● University develops partnerships that include joint conferences,
performances, research centres, and study programs.
● University overcomes the limitations in infrastructure and staﬃng shortages,

obstacles to professional development, cross-cultural training, advising, and IT.
Objectives
Federal University Dutse aims at nurturing and producing well-balanced men and
women, worthy in character and learning, who recognize and appreciate the dignity
of work, and are able to see themselves as agents of change for development and
for the common good. The University will concern itself with teaching, learning, and

research for the attainment of excellence, particularly in its chosen areas of teaching
and research to the end that it shall inculcate in its staff and students those higher
values which are fundamental to ethical, socio-economic, scientiﬁc and
technological development of the society. Five objectives that deﬁne the aspirations
and the key initiatives that will propel FUD on the path of prominence are:
1. To contribute to the National effort in providing high-quality university

education devoid of the usual interruptions.
2. To build an academic community in which the youths can develop their full
potential under a tradition of excellence in all spheres of life.
3. To advance the frontiers of knowledge through research in the various ﬁelds
of learning in which the university has expertise, especially Arts and the
Sciences.
4. To grow and develop into a signiﬁcant symbol of local and national
aspirations, especially for the host community, and to give and support

impeccable public service, and foster cooperation with private and public
institutions.
5. To prepare and motivate students to ﬁt into the world of knowledge, learning,
work, and service.
University Colours
The University's colours consist of Green and Gold. Green signiﬁes the university's
new and hopeful beginnings, including its agrarian setting. Gold is the ﬁgurative
expression of the university's climatic location in an arid zone, the Sahel, which is

deﬁned by the colour of sands and shifting dunes—an apt metaphor for the
university's vision of an unending quest for academic excellence and selﬂess service,
which are as valuable as Gold. Gold is also the colour of the distinctive soil of
Jigawa State, after which the State was named at its creation in 1991. These colours
also signify the endurance, courage, hope, and resilience of Sahel and Sudan
savannah life forms.
Faculty Colours

The eight Faculties of the University will have the following colours:
● Faculty of Agriculture: GREEN
● Faculty of Arts and Social Sciences: VIOLET
● Faculty of Computing: COFFEE BROWN
● Faculty of Education: PURPLE
● Faculty of Engineering: BROWN
● Faculty of Law: BLACK
● Faculty of Management Sciences: BLUE
● Faculty of Medicine: RED
● Faculty of Science: YELLOW
The colours will be reﬂected in the hoods and tassels of the respective faculties

which would be worn with the academic gown.
Motto
The Motto of FUD is: "Knowledge, Excellence, and Service". This is FUD's bold
declaration that it will be a university built upon local and universal knowledge;
knowledge that would be based on the best tradition of excellence which has guided
the best intellectual heritage of humankind; and knowledge and excellence premised
on public service, the noble aspiration for community, national, and universal

betterment, without which neither knowledge nor excellence as such would grow and
be relevant to human development. It is on these values that the University would
produce a new breed of graduates that would be dedicated to knowledgeable pursuit,
aspire to traditions of excellence in all respects, and accept selﬂess, disciplined, and
dedicated public service as the highest good.
The Logo
The Logo of FUD consists of an igneous rock-cropping (the dominant and most

abundant rock formation in Dutse, the capital of Jigawa State, and which deﬁnes
geologic identity within the Sudan savannah), a leaping gazelle (an animal native to
the land, which signiﬁes agility and resilience), and a date-palm tree (the dominant
and most abundant tree in Jigawa State, deﬁning its vegetation and ecology),
standing astride an open book setting atop the rock point, itself traversed by a pen
(both items signifying the greatest repositories of human knowledge, both religious

and secular—the open book of thought and life, and the pen a powerful symbol of
human intellectual technology).
Both these symbols are set against the background of a circular sphere fringed with
shades of green and beige borders (which denote the four elements of earthly life,
namely earth, ﬁre, water, and air), and all draped and framed by the primary colours
of Green and Light Brown. The Logo is signed off with the motto of the University,
"Knowledge, Excellence, and Service."

"Knowledge, Excellence, and Service."
The FUD Flag
The University Flag consists of the colours Green, White, and Beige (shown in a
tripartite colour structure) with the Logo emblazoned in the middle section of the
Flag.

Mascot (Emblem for FUD Sports)
The FUD Mascot (an emblem of its sports identity, as is the tradition in great
universities) is deﬁned by a camel, draped in green and golden brown colours. This
Mascot pays tribute to the people of the Sahel and Sudan Savannah, who have used
the camel for centuries as a means of transport, including annual sporting events
and other religious occasions such as the Sallah. As an emblematic character, the

Camel is a swift means of movement in the desert and is also a carrier of the special
hopes of the host communities of the University. The Camel is also a universal
symbol of awe-inspiring endurance and a veritable icon of arid life and ecological
uniqueness.
Location
The permanent site is located on the outskirts of Dutse Township along Ibrahim
Aliyu Bye-Pass. It consists of 500 hectares of virgin land. The Senate and almost all

the Faculties and departments are now operating at the permanent site.
ADMISSION REQUIREMENTS
UTME (JAMB) Requirements:
● Must choose FUD as ﬁrst choice institution
● Minimum JAMB score: 160 (general cut-off for 2025/2026)
● Minimum of 5 credits including English Language and Mathematics at O'Level
(WAEC/NECO), in not more than 2 sittings
● Must pass the Post-UTME screening (online)
Direct Entry (DE) Requirements:

Direct Entry (DE) Requirements:
● Minimum of 7 points in IJMB/NCE or Lower Credit at National Diploma (ND)
level
Post-UTME Process:
● Check admission status at the FUD PUTME portal: https://putme.fud.edu.ng
● Login with JAMB Number as username and State of Origin as password
● Pay acceptance fee after admission conﬁrmation

CHAPTER TWO: LOCATION OF THE UNIVERSITY
The Federal University Dutse is located in Dutse, the capital of Jigawa State. Jigawa
State itself was excised from Kano State on Tuesday, August 27, 1991. The State
covers a total land area of about 22,410 sq km. It is bordered on the West by Kano
State, on the East by Bauchi and Yobe States, and on the North by Katsina State and
the Republic of Niger.

the Republic of Niger.
With an estimated population of 17,697 according to the census conducted by the
National Population Commission (2006), Dutse is the fourth largest city in Jigawa
State, coming behind Hadejia, Gumel, and Birnin Kudu. The undulating relief of the
area is covered by Sudan savannah. Dutse became the capital of Jigawa State in
1991 when Jigawa State was split off from Kano State. Dutse is a serene town with

the intrinsic charm of small provincial towns that are largely free from the noise,
pollution, and squor of large metropolitan areas with expansive populations.
Rather, as we have seen, it has a modest population but is signiﬁcant in that,
although it is now undergoing its most sustained transformation into a modern
capital city—thanks to massive government investment in infrastructure and the
burgeoning Federal and State oﬃces and establishments within the township—Dutse

has still retained its proﬁle as a virtual holiday resort, offering great potential for
peace and quiet compared to Kano and Kaduna, for example.
There is also a rising presence of modern ﬁnancial centres such as banks and
insurance companies which are now taking advantage of the rising economic and
social proﬁle of the town. Real estate is a booming business, thanks to the inﬂux of
aﬄuent, middle-class people looking for economic and business opportunities in the
state capital.

state capital.
Brief History
Nevertheless, Dutse is not just a new administrative town but an ancient city. Local
legend based on oral tradition narrates its early story in the 13th century, when a
great hunter called Duna-Magu, possibly of Kanuri origins, ﬁrst named the settlement
"Dutse Gadawur". The settlement was then a rich hunting ground for gazelle (Hausa:
Gada) and other wild animals.
The settlement of Garu, which, according to oral tradition, started around the closing

years of the ﬁrst century A.D., was inhabited by a mixture of wandering tribes that
occupied the rock clusters in search of food and shelter. They found the surrounding
cluster of large boulders a safe hunting ground and at the same time ideal for
settlement. Jigawa, a hunting tribe, ﬁrst settled in the area and established their
dynasties in the thirteenth century. They were followed by Fulani immigrants from
Gazargamo, who settled in the area around Fagoji and who later conquered the

Hausa rulers and established their rule over the town in 1806.
Some informants suggest that the history of Dutse predates the 13th century
because of the town's proximity to Birnin Gija, a center of trans-Saharan commerce
that was as old as Kano. Birnin Gija is situated in the northern valley of Dutse and is
visible on clear days from the highest peaks of the surrounding rocks. The numerous
ancient dye pits that dot the landscape are still visible in many parts of the town.
Some scientists suggest that Dutse rock formations provided shelter for Stone Age

Humans. They base their belief on the discovery of stone tools around Garu and rock
paintings in Birnin Kudu. Whichever version of the story one believes, the earliest
written reference to Dutse appeared in the famous Kano Chronicle, which mentioned
its defeat by Sarkin Kano (Abdullahi Burja, c. 1438–1452) and his marriage to the
daughter of Sarkin Dutse Tuwai Mai Asarki Babba (c. 1421–1463).
The link between the earliest references to Dutse and local oral tradition listed twenty-

ﬁve other rulers of Dutse whose territorial acquisitions changed with individual war
skills or historical factors. The general belief was that almost all those who ruled
Dutse (1421–1553) were rulers of small settlements around Dutse and its immediate
environs, who took turns to govern the district under a special treaty.
Some historians suggest that they were one family but scattered around the hills in
smaller settlements such as Kachi, Takalaﬁya, Galamawa, Limawa, Gwari, Yina,

Maremawa, Gizirawa, Maranjuwa, and Jugurgur, or sometimes around water
sources, as their names suggest.
The earliest written reports indicated that by the opening years of the 18th century,
Dutse had grown into a large town comprising about seventy Wards within its walled
vicinity. The wall, which was built around the rock clusters enclosing the Jambo
stream within the central area, had twelve gates (Hausa: Kofoﬁn Gari) named after
the wards of the town nearest to the gates.
Topography

Topography
Dutse enjoys vast, fertile arable land to which almost all tropical crops could adapt,
thus constituting one of its highly prized natural resources. The Sudan Savannah
vegetation zone is also made up of vast grazing lands suitable for livestock
production. Major crops found in Dutse and other parts of the state include
groundnuts, sorghum, cotton, cowpeas, millet, and rice grown in the river valleys. The

herding of cattle, goats, and sheep is widespread. Jigawa State itself is crossed by
the main Lagos-Nguru railway and by a vast road network that links it to Kano and
Bauchi states.
The population of the state is a little over 4 million (according to the 2006 census).
The main rivers are the Hadejia, Kaﬁn Hausa, and Iggi, with a number of tributaries
feeding extensive marshlands in the north-eastern parts of the state. The Hadejia-

Kaﬁn Hausa River traverses the state from west to east through the Hadejia-Nguru
wetlands and eventually empties into the Lake Chad Basin.
Languages
There are four primary languages spoken in Dutse and Jigawa State as a whole:
Hausa, Fulfulde, Bade, Kanuri, and Warji. Hausa is the pre-eminent language spoken
as a native language by a large portion of Dutse inhabitants. The Hausa/Fulani
people are found in Dutse and all parts of Jigawa State. The Kanuri ethnic group is

largely found in Hadejia Emirate, with some traces of Badawa mainly in its north-
eastern parts. Even though each of the three dominant tribes has continued to
maintain its ethnic identity somewhat, Islam and a long history of intermarriage
serve as binding social factors.
Economy
Jigawa State has been largely characterized by informal sector activities, with
agriculture as the major economic activity. Over 80% of the population is engaged in

subsistence farming and animal husbandry. Trade and commerce are undertaken on
a small and medium scale, especially in agricultural goods, livestock, and other
consumer goods. Other informal sector activities include blacksmithing, leatherwork,
tailoring services, auto repairs, metalworks, carpentry, tanning, dyeing, food
processing, and masonry.
Even though the modern industrial and manufacturing sector is still relatively under-

developed, there are rising clusters of small-scale industries, particularly in areas of
food processing and other agro-allied activities in the larger population centers.
There are industrial enterprises in the state and these industries operate mostly with
raw materials produced locally, mainly from agriculture and livestock.
Communication and Transport
The capital city, Dutse, is served by an excellent metropolitan road network traversing

the township; there is an increasing presence of modern vehicular traﬃc such as
trucks, mini-buses, light cars, and motorcycles in the town. Yet the traditional ox and
cart still abound on the roads. The major GSM network providers maintain a steady
presence in Dutse, and it is expected that the town will be served by rapidly
expanding ICT facilities as it becomes more important administratively,
economically, and ﬁnancially.
Ethnic Composition

Ethnic Composition
Dutse, the immediate community of the Federal University Dutse, is mainly populated
by the Hausa/Fulani people. There are large concentrations of other settled ethnic
groups, both from within and outside Nigeria, living in Dutse and other Local
Government areas of the state.
Religion
Islam is the predominant religion of the people of Dutse and the state, with over
ninety-nine percent of the population being Muslim. There is a minority Christian

population, largely from settled and expatriate ethnic groups.
Culture
The rich cultural heritage of the people is symbolized by traditional modes of
dressing, music, dance, craftsmanship, and architecture. Hausa culture and
traditions are pre-eminent, although the Fulani, Mangawa, and Ngizimawa still
maintain aspects of their distinct cultures and traditions in terms of dress, food
processing, and settlement patterns within their indigenous areas.
Festivals

Festivals
The central, canonical festivals in Dutse and throughout the state are the Muslim
festivals of Eid-el-Fitr and Eid-el-Kabir (Sallah); these cut across ethnic barriers, being
a period of celebration for all Muslims across the world. Eid-el-Fitr is celebrated on
the 1st of Shawwal, the tenth month in the Islamic calendar, marking the end of the
Ramadan fasting period, while Eid-el-Kabir is marked on the 10th of Dhul-Hijjah, the

last month of the Islamic lunar calendar. These festivals are characterized by
colorful parades of gaily-dressed horse riders, camels, and various traditional
performances.
The 12th of Rabi'ul-Awwal, the 3rd month of the Islamic calendar, is marked as a day
for Maulud, commemorating the birth of Prophet Muhammad (PBUH). The

celebration is popularly tagged "Sallah Gani" in Hadejia and Gumel Emirates due to
the fanfare associated with it. Other festivals include Kakawa (traditional wrestling)
and Dambe (traditional boxing), mostly performed at the end of the harvest season.
The Sharo (traditional ﬂogging) is restricted to the Fulani community. Fishing
festivals also periodically take place in many communities.
Arts and Crafts
This area continues to play a dominant role in the socio-economic well-being of the

people by providing gainful employment and complementing the economic life of
farming families. Some of the arts and crafts include weaving (raﬃa and cloth),
pottery, blacksmithing, tanning, and leatherwork. Others include carving (of calabash
and wood), traditional textiles, and architecture.
Tourist Attractions in Dutse and Beyond
The tourism sector remains largely unexplored despite its vast potential to make
substantial contributions to economic and social development. Natural tourist

attractions and potential sites for resort developments abound in Dutse and Jigawa
State as a whole. Below are some of the most important ones:
● Baturiya Bird Sanctuary: Located in Kiri Kasamma Local Government area, it
covers an area of 320 sq km with a half-kilometer buffer zone surrounding it.
It provides a natural habitat for 378 species of migratory birds from places as
far as Europe and Australia. This site has been visited by Prince Philip and

Prince Charles, members of the worldwide Wildlife Conservation Fund.
● Wawan Raﬁ: Situated in Kazaure, this is a site for a holiday resort, golf
course, and other recreational facilities.
● Range of Quartzite Rocks: Located at Amaryawa in Roni Local Government
area, these rocks could be developed for mountaineering and as a picnic
resort centre.
● Ruined Unknown Town: Located in Gwaram Local Government Area; stone
walls and ancient houses still exist there.

walls and ancient houses still exist there.
● Location of the Emirs' Palaces: The Emirs' Palaces located at Dutse, Hadejia,
Kazaure, Gumel, and Ringim offer a combination of indigenous and modern
architecture.
● Captain Phillips' Tomb: Located at Hadejia. He led the battle between British
soldiers and the people of Hadejia in 1906.
● Fulani-Sharo: An annual performance by the Fulbe community.
● Market Days: Market days like Gujungu, Maigatari, Hadejia, Furji, Kazaure,

Gada, Babura, Sara, and Shuwarin host gatherings where thousands of people
gather to buy and sell commodities.
● Rock Paintings: The rock paintings of Birnin Kudu and the magniﬁcent rock
formations of Dutse are sites of great historical and geological interest.
CHAPTER THREE: UNIVERSITY GOVERNANCE
The main administrative structure of the University consists of the Visitor, the
Chancellor, the Council, the Senate, the Congregation, the Convocation, the Principal
Oﬃcers, and the Forum.

The Council
The Council is the highest policy-making body of the University, meeting at least four
times a year. The Council is the governing body of the University and has power,
under the University Act, to manage and adjudicate on matters regarding the
administration and ﬁnances of the University. The Council has powers to manage all
matters provided for by, or under, the University's laws. The Council shall, among
other things, supervise and control the University's ﬁnances, properties, and

privileges. The Council consists of the following membership:
1. Pro-Chancellor and Chairman
2. Vice-Chancellor
3. Deputy Vice-Chancellor(s)
4. Four members elected by Senate from among members of that body
5. Two members elected by the Congregation from among members of that
body
6. One member elected by the Convocation of the University from among
members of that body
7. Four members representing a variety of interests appointed by the Visitor

8. Representative of the Federal Ministry of Education
9. The Registrar of the University, who serves as the Secretary to the Council.
The Federal Government, as speciﬁed by the University Act, appoints the Chancellor
and the external members of Council.
The Senate
The Senate is presided over by the Vice-Chancellor. The Senate is the highest
academic authority and exercises control over matters relating to the academic

activities of the University, such as teaching, research, admissions, and student
discipline. The Senate has the following membership:
1. Vice-Chancellor
2. Deputy Vice-Chancellors
3. All Deans of Faculties, Postgraduate School, School of General Studies, and
Student Affairs
4. All Directors of Academic Units, Institutes, and Centres not below the rank of
Senior Lecturer
5. All Professors, subject to a maximum of two per department

6. Directors of various Institutes, Colleges, and Centres, and the University
Librarian
7. Heads of Academic Departments, not below the rank of Senior Lecturer.
Congregation
The Congregation is constituted with the Vice-Chancellor as Chairman, the Deputy
Vice-Chancellors, the University Librarian, the Bursar, and all members of academic,
administrative, and technical staff holding degrees conferred on them by recognized
Universities. The Registrar serves as the Secretary. The main function of

Congregation is to discuss matters of interest (both academic and non-academic) to
members of the University Community.
Convocation
The Convocation is made up of all staff of the University (both academic and non-
academic), as well as all graduates and students of the University. The Convocation
ceremony usually takes place every session. It is presided over by the Chancellor or,
in his absence, the Vice-Chancellor. At the Convocation ceremony, degree and

diploma certiﬁcates, as well as prizes, are awarded to deserving students. Honorary
Degrees are also awarded to distinguished personalities chosen by the University.
Upon the successful completion of their courses, students are expected to attend the
University Convocation during which they are formally awarded their degrees and
diplomas. Graduating students are notiﬁed of the convocation dates through media
outlets, National Youth Service Corps oﬃces, and their postal addresses if left

behind before leaving the university.
All graduates are requested to report to the University Campus prior to their
graduation ceremony for rehearsals and further instructions. Academic gowns are
available on loan and can be collected from the Faculty Oﬃcers of the respective
Faculties upon payment of a prescribed amount. Graduates of the University are
entitled to join its Alumni Association by application for registration and payment of

prescribed fees. For more information, contact the Student Affairs Division or the
Alumni Relations Unit of the Registrar's oﬃce.
UNIVERSITY ADMINISTRATION
● Oﬃce of the Vice-Chancellor: The Oﬃce of the Vice-Chancellor is the nerve
centre of the University's administration. The Vice-Chancellor is the Chief
Executive Oﬃcer of the University. For this reason, certain key sectors of the
University administration are placed directly under his oﬃce for direct control

and supervision. These sectors include Works and Maintenance, Security,
Physical Planning, Academic Planning, Audit, Fire Service, Information, Health
Services and Sanitation, Advancement & Alumni Relations, and Student
Affairs. Two Deputy Vice-Chancellors—one for Central Administration and the
other for Academic Services—assist the Vice-Chancellor in the discharge of
his duties.
● Oﬃce of the Registrar: The Registrar is the principal administrative oﬃcer of

the University. He is directly responsible to the Vice-Chancellor for the day-to-
day administration of the University. His oﬃce, known as the Registry,
provides secretariat services to all Units of the University. In addition, as the
Chief Administrative Oﬃcer of the University, the Registrar is the Secretary of
Council, Senate, and Congregation. His oﬃce handles all formal
correspondence on appointments, resignations, contracts, supplies, legal, and

property matters. The Registry contains a number of Divisions, including the
Establishment Oﬃce, Academic Oﬃce, Council & General Administration,
Development, and Liaison oﬃces.
● Oﬃce of the Bursar: The Bursar handles all ﬁnancial matters of the
University. His oﬃce, known as the Bursary, handles purchases, stores of
goods, and keeps records of the University's accounts. He is directly

responsible to the Vice-Chancellor.
● The University Librarian: The University Librarian, as the Administrative and
Academic head of the Library, is responsible to the Vice-Chancellor for the
orderly growth and development of the University Library and the coordination
of library services across the University, its faculties, departments, and other
teaching and research units.

Objectives of the Library

a) To provide materials in support of teaching and research within the learning
process. This includes materials for student coursework, assigned reading, and
background reading for essays, term papers, and projects.
b) To provide materials to meet the requirements of faculty specialists, postgraduate
students, and other researchers.
c) To provide materials to assist library users in their personal self-development.

d) To cooperate with other university libraries with a view to developing a network of
academic library resources.
e) To meet the specialized information needs of the community within which the
University is situated.
f) Guided by these objectives, the library will acquire materials at ﬁve levels:
Remedial, Undergraduate, Postgraduate/Research, General Studies, and Light/
Specialized information. These materials include books, journals, periodicals, and
electronically stored information.

electronically stored information.
Administration of the Library
The University Librarian, who heads a team of qualiﬁed professional staff,
administers the Library. There are seven operational divisions in the library:
1. The Collection Development Division: Charged with the responsibility of
developing the total book collection of the library and its branches.
2. The Technical Services Division: Responsible for processing acquired books

to enable readers to gain access to them on the shelves with minimum delay.
This Division maintains a card catalogue indicating what the library holds
and where items are located on the shelves.
3. The Readers Services Division: The public relations organ that registers
readers and controls the use of Library materials. It ensures library rules and
regulations are obeyed and assists readers optimally in their reference and

bibliographic requests. It also provides closed access service at the reserve
unit, manages charging and discharging of users, and collects ﬁnes when
library books are overdue.
4. The Educational and Information Division: Manages core library instruction
courses, library publications, and seminars, and helps provide general
information about the library.
5. The Serials Division: Acquires scientiﬁc and other materials published

regularly or semi-regularly. These materials discuss current issues and
developments on given subjects. The acquisition, processing, and public
services rendered by this Division are of immense beneﬁt to students,
academic staff, and researchers.
Collection Development
Apart from developing library stock to a reasonable level, for which an acquisition
policy must be made, the library relates its procurement of materials to the total

research and "Centre of Excellence" needs of the University.

Registration

● Undergraduate Students: 3 cards

Desk. The cost of replacement is determined by the University Librarian.
● The use of materials obtained through inter-library loan services is governed
by the regulations of the lending institution.

by the regulations of the lending institution.
● Photocopying services are offered in accordance with copyright laws and are
executed by authorized staff. Materials can be submitted and collected
between 9:00 am and 3:00 pm, Monday to Friday, at rates determined by the
University Librarian.
● Smoking, eating, and drinking are strictly prohibited in the Library.
● Children and animals are prohibited from entering the Library, except under

special circumstances or with the permission of the University Librarian (e.g.,
guide dogs for the blind).
● The Librarian assumes no responsibility for personal property lost on the
premises.
● Users must not reserve seats. Seats left empty may be taken by other readers.
● Users should leave all consulted books and journals on the tables and must
not attempt to re-shelve them.
● Stealing, mutilating, or losing library materials attracts disciplinary action,

including paying ﬁve times the current value of the book or replacing the exact
book within two days.
Library operations are computerized using an integrated library automation system.
The system allows for rapid access to the library's catalogue both from within the
library and remotely via the internet. Its 'broadcast' search facility provides direct
access and simultaneous searching of selected online catalogues of other libraries.

INTERNET SERVICES

● As an expert system, giving access to specialists across thousands of ﬁelds.

CHAPTER FIVE: STUDENT AFFAIRS DIVISION
Introduction
The Student Affairs Division is in charge of all student services except academic
matters. The Division operates under the Oﬃce of the Vice-Chancellor.

Administration
The Dean of Student Affairs, who is a senior member of the academic staff, heads
the Division. He is appointed by the Vice-Chancellor and is directly responsible to him
for the day-to-day running of the Division and the conduct of student affairs. The
Dean is assisted by at least a Deputy. The Student Affairs Oﬃcer, a senior member
of the administrative staff from the Registry, handles the secretarial work of the
Division.
Oﬃce of the Dean

Division.
Oﬃce of the Dean
The Dean is the overall Chief Executive of the Division. It is his responsibility to
coordinate all non-academic affairs of the students. He is responsible for student
welfare and discipline. He is the accounting oﬃcer and controls the Division's votes
and vehicles. He is responsible for accommodating students in the halls of
residence. He represents the Division in all matters affecting students' welfare and

discipline, and liaises with outside bodies on these matters.
He compiles and keeps records of vital statistical information on students (e.g.,
name, age, gender, year of registration, duration of course, contact address, next of
kin, nationality, state, local government, marital status, and sponsorship). He
presides over committees located within the Division and submits reports to the Vice-
Chancellor.
Central Administration

Chancellor.
Central Administration
The Student Affairs Oﬃcer (SAO) heads the central administration and coordinates
the administrative work of the Division. Under the direction of the Dean, the SAO
handles Student Union matters, coordinates orientation programmes, and handles
matters relating to the NYSC and foreign students. The SAO identiﬁes students for
the issuance of passports, visas, and rebates, signs postal/money orders for

students, and acts as the hospitality and protocol oﬃcer of the Division.
Career Guidance and Counseling
The Guidance and Human Resource Development Unit is headed by the Guidance
and Counselling Oﬃcer, who reports to the Dean through the Student Affairs Oﬃcer.
The Unit provides advice on employment matters, organizes career lectures and
excursions, assists students in securing vacation jobs, and coordinates interviews
with outside agencies for scholarships and job placements.
The Guidance Counselor identiﬁes students for scholarships and bursaries, collects

and distributes relevant forms, and prepares conﬁdential tracking reports. The unit
also provides counseling services to students dealing with health-related or personal
problems.
Student Relation and Hall Administration
The Student Relation Oﬃcer manages this unit and reports to the Dean through the
Student Affairs Oﬃcer. This oﬃcer liaises with the Works and Maintenance
Department to manage hostel repairs, sanitation, and aesthetics. The unit enforces

all rules governing hostel accommodation and manages records of available rooms,
bed spaces, and occupant characteristics, while overseeing student activities such
as meetings and publications.
Sports Council
The Chairman of the University Sports Council heads this unit, which is responsible
for providing sporting facilities and equipment. It coordinates sporting competitions,
intramural games, and external athletic events.

intramural games, and external athletic events.
The members of the Sports Council include: The Vice-Chancellor or his nominee
(Chairman); Head of the Department of Physical and Health Education; Director of
University Health Services; Director of Works; Director of Sports (Student Union
Government); Director of the Physical Planning Unit; two Senate representatives; one
Congregation representative; and three students nominated by the Dean of Student

Affairs (one of whom must be female). The Secretary is the University Chief Coach.
Quorum is formed by the Chairman and one-third of the members.
Terms of reference include advising the Vice-Chancellor on athletic policy, acting as
the governing body for student sports, preparing the sports budget, and arbitrating
athletic disputes.
Student Welfare Unit
The Welfare Oﬃcer handles students' social activities and entertainment, alongside

critical welfare matters such as bereavements, ﬁre outbreaks, ﬂooding, food
poisoning, epidemic control, hostel water and electricity supplies, and resolving
internal student disputes.
Hall Administration
The residential system consists of Halls and Hostel Blocks named after illustrious
Nigerians. Each Hall has a Hall Master and a Warden. A Hall Oﬃce Committee,
comprising hall oﬃcials and elected student representatives, manages localized

social, athletic, and intellectual operations. Hall Masters and Wardens conduct daily
visits to attend to student needs.
Private Hostel Development
The University liaises with relevant agencies to provide hostels. While initial phases
aim for maximum coverage, long-term policy targets accommodating at least thirty
percent (30%) of the student population on campus. Students allocated spaces pay
accommodation fees and hostel maintenance charges. The University utilizes Build-

Operate-Transfer partnerships with private developers, cooperative societies, and
state governments to expand residential capacity using available university land.
Committees Located in the Division

To provide general guidance on the conduct of Student Affairs, three high-powered
committees were established by the Senate:
1. Committee of Student Affairs
2. Committee of Hall Masters and Hall Wardens
3. Hall Committees
● The Student Affairs Committee: Advises on policies relating to non-academic
student affairs. Membership includes the Vice-Chancellor or nominee
(Chairman), Dean of Student Affairs, Hall Masters/Mistresses/Wardens,

Registrar, Bursar, Director of Works, Chief Medical Oﬃcer, Chief Security
Oﬃcer, two Council representatives, two Senate representatives, one ASUU
representative, one Local Community representative, and two student
representatives. The Student Affairs Oﬃcer serves as Secretary. Quorum
requires the Chairman and eleven members.
● Committee of Hall Masters and Hall Wardens: Recommends structural
actions to the Vice-Chancellor regarding student welfare, hall discipline, and

inter-hall sports. Membership includes the Vice-Chancellor or nominee
(Chairman), Dean of Student Affairs, Hall Masters, Deputy Hall Masters, Hall
Wardens, and Assistant Hall Wardens. Elected student Hall Chairmen attend
but do not vote. Quorum requires the Chairman and eight members.
● Hall Committees: Responsible for localized social, athletic, and intellectual
activities intended to enrich life within individual halls. It consists of the Hall

Master (Chairman), Assistant Hall Master, Warden, Assistant Warden, Hall
Oﬃcer, and elected student representatives. It meets monthly and feeds
recommendations to the central committee.
Core Activities of the Division
● Orientation of New Students: Organized at the start of the session to help
fresh students adjust to University life. Activities include meeting the Vice-
Chancellor and senior oﬃcers, alongside introductions to library and IT

facilities. Fresh students must pay fees and register online before they can be
allocated hostel spaces.
● Matriculation: Every new student must take the matriculation oath to be
formally recognized as a member of the academic community. This ceremony
marks the oﬃcial closing of admissions for that session. Gowns must be
worn, the Dean presents the students, and the Registrar administers the oath.
● National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) Mobilization: Established by Decree

No. 24 of 1973, the scheme mandates that every Nigerian citizen who
completes a ﬁrst degree before the age of 30 must participate in a service
year. Graduates are deployed to states other than their state of origin, with
concessions made only for military/security personnel, those over 30, the
chronically ill, or married women with established family responsibilities.
o Online Registration: Prospective corps members must register online for

mobilization upon graduation, in coordination with the Student Affairs
Division.
o Discipline: Corps members operate under a strict code of conduct and

NYSC bye-laws, with performance evaluated quarterly by their assigned
deployment heads.
CHAPTER SIX: STUDENT SERVICES
Accommodation Regulations
Hostel accommodation is a privilege and is strictly voluntary. Spaces are allocated
based on availability and speciﬁc health/sanitation conditions.
1. Squatting is Illegal: Harboring unauthorized persons is strictly prohibited.
Violators and their guests face immediate eviction from the hall and referral to

the Student Disciplinary Committee. Students whose families reside in Dutse
are discouraged from applying.
2. Check-In/Check-Out System: Allocated students inspect room facilities
alongside the Porter and sign a facility tracking card. Upon vacating the room
during holidays or graduation, the room is checked against the card. Students
are billed for any missing or damaged ﬁxtures.
3. Submission of Property: Keys, mattresses, and pillows must be returned to

the Porter at the end of the session. Leftover personal property is kept at the
owner's risk.
4. Vacation Residence: Staying in the hostels during vacations requires written
authorization from the Dean of Student Affairs based on veriﬁed academic or
institutional needs.
5. Cooking Prohibited: Cooking inside study rooms is strictly banned.
Kitchenettes are provided speciﬁcally for this purpose.
6. Room Transfer Bans: Exchanging, selling, or subletting an allocated bed

space is a serious offense. Violations result in immediate revocation of the
allocation, forcible ejection, and a one-session suspension for all parties
involved.
7. Pregnancy Policy: Pregnant students will NOT be provided with
accommodation in the university hostels.
8. General Prohibitions:
o Candles are completely barred due to ﬁre risks; only battery-operated
ﬂashlights are permitted during blackouts. Electric stoves, hotplates,
and ring boilers are banned from bedrooms.

and ring boilers are banned from bedrooms.
o Pets of any kind are prohibited.
o The sale, purchase, or consumption of alcohol, liquors, and illicit drugs is
strictly prohibited on campus.
o Open defecation, bathing, or urinating outside designated bathrooms
and toilets is a punishable offense.
o Washing and drying clothes must be limited to designated laundry lines.
o Handsets, laptops, and tablets are kept at the owner's risk; losses due to

student negligence will not be investigated as institutional liabilities.
Visitor Rules
● Visitors must sign the visitor's book at the porter's lodge upon arrival and

departure.
● Female visitors are completely barred from entering male hostels/rooms,
and male visitors are similarly barred from female hostels at all hours.
Visitors must be received within the designated Common Rooms.
● Overnight guests are strictly prohibited.
● Visitors are subject to security searches at the university gates and must
observe campus speed limits.
Grievance Channels
Minor residential complaints should be addressed ﬁrst to the Head Porter, then

escalated to the Hall Warden, Hall Master, and the Dean of Student Affairs. If
unresolved, issues go to the Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Central Administration).
Students are strictly forbidden from bypassing this hierarchy to contact the Vice-
Chancellor directly.
Medical Services
The University Clinic provides healthcare services staffed by a Medical Oﬃcer and
nursing personnel. Students must register with the Clinic immediately after

completing academic registration to qualify for free treatments. Students with
chronic conditions (e.g., asthma, diabetes) must register their status to facilitate
emergency care.
● Medical Insurance (NHIS): In line with the National Health Insurance Scheme
Act No. 35 of 1999, students contribute a premium during registration to
secure primary healthcare coverage for two semesters. The program covers
ambulance evacuations, emergency treatments, routine laboratory tests,

standard dental care, simple surgeries, eye tests, and basic obstetric care.
● Student Accident Insurance Policy: A mandatory ₦500.00 annual premium
provides comprehensive cover for traﬃc accidents, injuries, permanent
disabilities, or natural death occurring during the academic session.
o Compensations: Permanent disability or heavy medical expenses are
covered up to ₦100,000.00. Death beneﬁts pay out ₦200,000.00 to the
registered next of kin upon presentation of oﬃcial death certiﬁcates or

reports from religious ministers.
Financial and Commercial Services
● Collections: Only authorized clubs and societies may collect membership
subscriptions. General charitable collections require explicit permission from
the Vice-Chancellor.
● Catering & Bookstore: Private restaurants operate on campus under hygiene
standards monitored by the Student Affairs Division. Private bookstores
provide text books, stationery, photocopying services, and oﬃcial university
souvenirs.

souvenirs.
● Banking: The campus hosts formal banking structures and ATM points.
Students are strongly advised to maintain electronic bank accounts and avoid
carrying large cash balances.
Student Benevolent Fund

Managed by a Board of Trustees chaired by the Vice-Chancellor, this fund uses a
portion of registration fees to provide emergency relief, micro-credits, and specialized
welfare grants to indigent or distressed students. Application forms are available via
the Student Union Government (SUG) President or the Student Affairs Oﬃcer.

CHAPTER EIGHT: ASSOCIATIONS, CLUBS, AND SOCIETIES
All student organizations operate under the strict oversight of the Student Affairs
Division.
Registered Categories
1. Religious Associations: In accordance with Section 38 (1) of the Nigerian
Constitution, freedom of thought, conscience, and religion is protected.

Currently, three central religious bodies are recognized: The Fellowship of
Christian Students (FCS) for Protestant denominations, the National
Federation of Catholic Students (NFCS), and the Muslim Students Society of
Nigeria (MSSN). Membership is strictly voluntary. The University reserves the
right to deny registration to any group that threatens campus peace.
2. Departmental / Faculty Associations: Formed around distinct academic
disciplines to promote scientiﬁc and research activities.

3. State Associations: Established to manage common welfare goals, cultural
preservation, and state-level scholarship coordination. All ethnic sub-groups
must operate under the umbrella of their respective State Associations.
4. Service, Sporting, and Social Clubs: Humanitarian, interest-based, or athletic
associations. A list of approved, cleared social clubs is updated periodically.
5. Press Clubs: Published campus newsletters serve as channels for expression

and behavior guidance. Editorial lines are monitored by assigned Staff
Advisers to ensure decorum.

Registration Guidelines and Procedures
● A minimum of 25 registered students (excluding executives) is required to
form an association.
● Organizations must maintain at least three Staff Advisers drawn from
university personnel, at least one of whom must hold the minimum rank of a
Senior Lecturer.
● Financial Accountability: All clubs must submit a comprehensive annual
ﬁnancial report and an activity report endorsed by their Staff Advisers to
renew their registration.

renew their registration.
● Off-Campus Guests: Inviting high-proﬁle external speakers (e.g., Governors,
Ministers, Ambassadors) requires submitting Form "G" to the Dean of Student
Affairs at least seven days in advance for security and protocol clearing.
● Night Activities: Student functions are barred from running late into the
night. Special exceptions must occur in approved venues and must terminate
by 12:00 midnight.
CHAPTER NINE: THE STUDENT UNION

CHAPTER NINE: THE STUDENT UNION
The Student Union operates under a constitution approved by the University Senate.
It focuses on facilitating the social, athletic, and cultural integration of the student
body. The Student Union is not a trade union and is strictly barred from participating
in external trade unionism or labor disputes. Membership is completely voluntary.
Union Structure
● The Congress: The general body comprising all registered, interested student
members.

members.
● The Parliament (Students' Representative Assembly - SRA): The supreme
legislative body made up of elected representatives from various halls. It must
ratify all major budget allocations and decisions proposed by the Executive.
● The Executive Council: Comprises the elected oﬃcers:
1. President
2. Vice President
3. Secretary-General
4. Assistant Secretary-General
5. Treasurer
6. Financial Secretary
7. Public Relations Oﬃcer
8. Welfare Oﬃcer
9. Director of Socials

8. Welfare Oﬃcer
9. Director of Socials
10. Director of Sports
Financial Governance
The Union is funded via subscription fees collected by the university at registration.
Funds are deposited into a dedicated bank account. Signatories to the account are:

The Chairman of the Student Affairs Committee, the Student Union President, and
the Student Union Treasurer. Expenditure vouchers require supporting parliamentary
approvals and must be endorsed by the Dean of Student Affairs. Accounts are
audited periodically by an independent student-and-staff audit team.
Leadership Eligibility Rules
1. A candidate contesting for any oﬃce within the Student Union must
maintain a minimum Cumulative Grade Point Average (CGPA) of 3.00.

2. A student cannot hold oﬃce in the Student Union while simultaneously
executing an executive role in another campus or external association.
3. Candidates who contest for an Executive Council seat and lose are barred
from entering the SRA during the same academic session.
4. Students found guilty of disciplinary infractions or exam malpractice by any
university organ are permanently disqualiﬁed from running for oﬃce.

5. The university will withhold Certiﬁcates of Honour from any union executive
who resigns under controversial circumstances or is removed via
impeachment.
Demonstration Framework
● No group or sub-association other than the oﬃcial Student Union has the
right to organize a demonstration on campus.
● The Student Union Executive must issue a formal 72-hour written notice to
the Registrar, channeled through the Dean of Student Affairs, before
embarking on any planned demonstration.
